items.title: VerdantBamboo: Just Another BRICKSTORM in the Firewall
items.description: Chinese threat actor VerdantBamboo compromised a victim organization and its Managed Services Provider over an 18-month period, deploying malware on network edge devices lacking EDR coverage. The initial breach involved an Egnyte Storage Sync system, where attackers exploited a sudo misconfiguration for privilege escalation and installed BRICKSTORM backdoor and AGENTPSD fallback implant. Investiga
items.ai_summary: The VerdantBamboo threat actor exploited a sudo misconfiguration on an Egnyte Storage Sync system to gain initial access, leveraging T1068 for privilege escalation. This allowed them to install the BRICKSTORM backdoor and AGENTPSD fallback implant on network edge devices, which lacked Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) coverage. The real-world impact includes the compromise of a victim organization and its Managed Services Provider over an 18-month period, with potential data breaches and unauthorized access. To remediate, defenders should ensure proper configuration of sudo privileges, implement EDR solutions on all network devices, and monitor for suspicious activity related to T1649 and T1219. Specifically, defenders should review their Egnyte Storage Sync systems for any signs of unauthorized access or malware installation, and update their systems to the latest version with secure configuration settings.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1068| B[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    B -->|T1649| C[Credential Access]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Remote Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Miasma Worm Campaign Spreads with New PyPI Wave
items.description: A coordinated PyPI compromise campaign involving 37 malicious wheel artifacts across 19 packages was detected, utilizing Python startup hooks to execute credential-stealing payloads. The attack leverages .pth files for automatic execution during Python interpreter startup, downloads the Bun JavaScript runtime, and runs obfuscated JavaScript payloads. The malware targets high-value developer and CI
items.ai_summary: The Miasma Worm Campaign is a coordinated PyPI compromise campaign that involves 37 malicious wheel artifacts across 19 packages, utilizing Python startup hooks to execute credential-stealing payloads. The attack leverages .pth files for automatic execution during Python interpreter startup, downloads the Bun JavaScript runtime, and runs obfuscated JavaScript payloads. This campaign targets high-value developer and CI environments, potentially leading to significant data breaches and unauthorized access. To remediate, defenders should immediately inspect their Python environments for suspicious .pth files and remove any malicious packages, then update their package lists to ensure they are using the latest secure versions. Additionally, implementing strict access controls and monitoring for unusual JavaScript runtime executions can help prevent further exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059.007| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    C -->|T1550| D[Credential Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Technical Advisory: Breach of Instructure Canvas LMS
items.description: In early May 2026, Instructure confirmed a breach affecting its Canvas learning platform after detecting unauthorized activity on May 1. ShinyHunters exploited the Free-For-Teacher account program, compromising the Canvas platform directly and exposing names, email addresses, student IDs, and private messages. The exposure window ran from April 30 to May 7, 2026. ShinyHunters claims 3.6 TB of data
items.ai_summary: The breach of Instructure Canvas LMS, attributed to the threat actor ShinyHunters, exploited the Free-For-Teacher account program, resulting in the exposure of sensitive information including names, email addresses, student IDs, and private messages. This incident highlights the importance of securing educational platforms, as the compromised data can be used for phishing, social engineering, or other malicious activities. The exposure window, which lasted from April 30 to May 7, 2026, indicates a relatively short period of unauthorized access, but the sheer volume of exposed data, claimed to be 3.6 TB, underscores the severity of the breach. To mitigate similar incidents, educational institutions using the Canvas platform should review their account management practices, particularly for free or trial accounts, and implement additional security measures such as multi-factor authentication and regular security audits. Furthermore, users of the platform should be vigilant about suspicious activities and report any unusual account behavior to the administrators.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1615| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Exfiltration]:::tactic
    D -->|T1083| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Fighting Spyware: An Update
items.description: WhatsApp successfully identified and disrupted spear phishing attempts linked to NSO Group, a spyware firm blacklisted by the US government. The company is requesting the court to hold NSO in contempt for violating a permanent injunction that prohibited them from targeting WhatsApp and its users. The attacks involved social engineering attempts to trick users into clicking malicious links, as well
items.ai_summary: The threat involves spear phishing attempts linked to NSO Group, a spyware firm, targeting WhatsApp users through social engineering tactics. The attack vector involves tricking users into clicking malicious links, exploiting user execution vulnerabilities (T1204). The real-world impact of this threat is significant, as successful exploitation can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive user data. To remediate this threat, WhatsApp users should be cautious when clicking links from unknown sources and ensure they are using the latest version of the application. Additionally, users can enable two-factor authentication and keep their devices and software up to date to reduce the risk of exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades Worms Target Bioinformatics and MCP Developers via Malicious PyPI Wheels
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack campaign has expanded to 471 affected artifacts across npm and PyPI, targeting developers through malicious packages. The campaign uses three distinct delivery methods: executable .pth startup hooks, trojanized native .abi3.so extensions that execute at import time, and a split loader-payload architecture that searches Python's sys.path. Twenty-three newly ident
items.ai_summary: The Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades Worms campaign is a sophisticated supply chain attack targeting bioinformatics and MCP developers through malicious PyPI wheels. The attack uses three distinct delivery methods, including executable .pth startup hooks, trojanized native .abi3.so extensions, and a split loader-payload architecture that searches Python's sys.path. If exploited, this vulnerability could lead to unauthorized access and data breaches, affecting developers who have installed the malicious packages. To remediate, developers should remove any suspicious packages and update their PyPI dependencies to the latest versions, ensuring that their Python environments are configured to use secure package sources. Additionally, developers should monitor their systems for unusual activity and implement robust authentication mechanisms to prevent unauthorized access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    B -->|T1550| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A First Look at a New Post-Exploitation Red Team Tool
items.description: A new post-exploitation red team tool named Splinter has been discovered on customer systems through Advanced WildFire's memory scanning capabilities. Developed in Rust programming language, Splinter is exceptionally large at around 7MB due to statically linked libraries. The tool uses a JSON configuration structure containing implant ID, C2 server details, and operational parameters. It operates
items.ai_summary: The newly discovered post-exploitation red team tool, Splinter, is a large 7MB tool developed in Rust, utilizing a JSON configuration structure to store implant ID, C2 server details, and operational parameters. Splinter operates using the Application Layer Protocol, as indicated by the detected TTP T1071, allowing it to potentially evade detection and facilitate communication with its command and control server. The real-world impact of Splinter includes the potential for unauthorized access and data exfiltration, affecting organizations that have not implemented robust post-exploitation detection and response measures. To remediate, defenders should implement memory scanning capabilities, such as those provided by Advanced WildFire, and monitor for unusual network activity, specifically focusing on application layer protocols. Additionally, defenders should ensure their systems are configured to detect and block large, statically linked binaries like Splinter.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1071| B[Command and Control]:::tactic
    B -->|T1041| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: AI brands as bait: How threat actors are using the AI hype in social engineering
items.description: Threat actors are increasingly leveraging the global interest in artificial intelligence by impersonating popular AI platforms such as ChatGPT, Copilot, DeepSeek, and Claude in social engineering campaigns. These operations span phishing attacks, malvertising, and search engine optimization-driven tactics that ultimately lead to credential theft, financial fraud, or malware infections. Observed ca
items.ai_summary: Threat actors are leveraging the global interest in artificial intelligence to conduct social engineering campaigns, impersonating popular AI platforms such as ChatGPT, Copilot, DeepSeek, and Claude. These campaigns involve phishing attacks, malvertising, and search engine optimization-driven tactics, ultimately leading to credential theft, financial fraud, or malware infections. The attack vector typically involves phishing emails or malicious advertisements that trick users into divulging sensitive information or executing malicious code. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering solutions, such as those that utilize machine learning algorithms to detect phishing attempts, and ensure that all employees undergo regular security awareness training to recognize and report suspicious emails or advertisements. Additionally, defenders should consider implementing a web application firewall to detect and prevent drive-by compromise attacks, and ensure that all systems and software are up-to-date with the latest security patches.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1189| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1204| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1583.008| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Hacktivists are broadening their scope beyond political motivation
items.description: Kaspersky researchers uncovered interconnected hacktivist campaigns attributed to groups including 4BID, Hakerskii Kit, and C.A.S., targeting organizations primarily in Russia and Belarus, but expanding to Kazakhstan, UAE, Syria, and Egypt. Attackers exploited ProxyShell vulnerabilities in Microsoft Exchange servers to deploy fd.aspx web shells and various post-exploitation frameworks including Sl
items.ai_summary: The hacktivist groups, including 4BID, Hakerskii Kit, and C.A.S., have been targeting organizations primarily in Russia and Belarus, but are expanding their scope to other countries such as Kazakhstan, UAE, Syria, and Egypt. The attackers exploited ProxyShell vulnerabilities in Microsoft Exchange servers, which allowed them to deploy fd.aspx web shells and various post-exploitation frameworks, including Sl. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to unauthorized access to sensitive data and disruption of services. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure their Microsoft Exchange servers are updated with the latest security patches and configure their systems to restrict access to the fd.aspx file. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and implement a web application firewall to detect and prevent similar attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1505| B[Server Software Component]:::tactic
    B -->|T1491| C[Defacement]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Don't Fear the Repo: UNK_DeadDrop Phishing Campaign Targets Developers to Steal Cryptocurrency
items.description: Between April and May 2026, a likely North Korean threat actor conducted phishing campaigns targeting developers across nearly 100 organizations in finance, cryptocurrency, education, and technology sectors. The attacks used recruitment and code review themes, delivering emails with links to actor-controlled GitHub repositories hosting malicious scripts. The infection chain exploited Visual Studio
items.ai_summary: The UNK_DeadDrop phishing campaign, attributed to a likely North Korean threat actor, targeted developers across nearly 100 organizations in finance, cryptocurrency, education, and technology sectors between April and May 2026. The campaign used recruitment and code review themes to deliver emails with links to actor-controlled GitHub repositories hosting malicious scripts, exploiting Visual Studio. If exploited, the campaign could lead to the theft of cryptocurrency, with real-world impact including financial loss for affected individuals and organizations. To remediate, defenders should implement email filters to block suspicious links, educate developers on safe coding practices, and monitor GitHub repositories for malicious activity. Additionally, developers should verify the authenticity of code reviews and recruitment emails, and avoid clicking on links from unknown sources.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Old WinRAR Flaw Fuels Attacks on Ukraine: How Unmanaged Software Keeps the Door Open
items.description: Two Russia-aligned campaigns continue exploiting CVE-2025-8088, a WinRAR path traversal vulnerability patched in July 2025, against Ukrainian organizations through April 2026. SHADOW-EARTH-066 deploys an evolved GIFTEDCROOK information stealer using in-memory DLL loading via direct NT system calls, harvesting browser credentials, session cookies, and documents across 35 file extensions before self
items.ai_summary: The CVE-2025-8088 vulnerability in WinRAR is being exploited by Russia-aligned threat actors in campaigns targeting Ukrainian organizations. This path traversal vulnerability, patched in July 2025, allows attackers to execute arbitrary code, leading to the deployment of the GIFTEDCROOK information stealer. The stealer uses in-memory DLL loading via direct NT system calls to harvest sensitive data, including browser credentials, session cookies, and documents across 35 file extensions. To remediate, defenders should ensure all WinRAR installations are updated to a version released after the July 2025 patch, and consider implementing application whitelisting to restrict unauthorized software execution. Additionally, organizations should monitor for suspicious activity related to web session cookie theft and credentials exfiltration from web browsers.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1555.003| C[Credentials from Web Browsers]:::tactic
    C -->|T1539| D[Steal Web Session Cookie]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2018-25412: Delta Sql 1.8.2 contains an arbitrary file upload vulnerability that allows unau...
items.description: Delta Sql 1.8.2 contains an arbitrary file upload vulnerability that allows unauthenticated attackers to upload malicious files by sending POST requests to docs_upload.php with crafted multipart form data. Attackers can upload PHP files with arbitrary content to the upload directory and execute them on the server for remote code execution.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2018-25412 is a critical remote code execution (RCE) vulnerability in Delta SQL 1.8.2, allowing unauthenticated attackers to upload malicious PHP files via crafted multipart form data to the docs_upload.php endpoint. This vulnerability can be exploited by sending POST requests to the vulnerable endpoint, which can lead to server-side RCE. The affected product is Delta SQL 1.8.2, and the attack vector is a public-facing application. To remediate this vulnerability, update to Delta SQL 1.8.3 or later, which patches the vulnerability. Additionally, ensure that the upload directory is properly configured to prevent arbitrary file uploads.

items.title: CVE-2018-25411: MGB OpenSource Guestbook 0.7.0.2 contains an SQL injection vulnerability that al...
items.description: MGB OpenSource Guestbook 0.7.0.2 contains an SQL injection vulnerability that allows unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL queries by injecting malicious code through the 'id' parameter. Attackers can send GET requests to email.php with crafted SQL payloads in the 'id' parameter to extract sensitive database information including table and column names.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2018-25411 is an SQL injection vulnerability in MGB OpenSource Guestbook 0.7.0.2, allowing unauthenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL queries by injecting malicious code through the 'id' parameter in the 'email.php' page. This vulnerability can be exploited by sending GET requests with crafted SQL payloads in the 'id' parameter, enabling attackers to extract sensitive database information, including table and column names. To remediate this vulnerability, update to MGB OpenSource Guestbook 0.7.0.3 or later, which patches the SQL injection vulnerability. Additionally, ensure that the 'id' parameter is properly sanitized and validated to prevent similar attacks. This vulnerability has a real-world impact, as demonstrated by its CVSS score of 8.2, indicating a high severity rating.

items.title: CVE-2018-25410: SIM-PKH 2.4.1 contains an SQL injection vulnerability that allows authenticated ...
items.description: SIM-PKH 2.4.1 contains an SQL injection vulnerability that allows authenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL queries by injecting malicious code through the 'id' parameter. Attackers can send GET requests to /admin/media.php with module=pengurus and act=editpengurus parameters containing SQL UNION statements to extract database information including usernames, database names, and version det
items.ai_summary: CVE-2018-25410 is an SQL injection vulnerability in SIM-PKH 2.4.1, allowing authenticated attackers to execute arbitrary SQL queries by injecting malicious code through the 'id' parameter in the /admin/media.php page. Attackers can send GET requests with SQL UNION statements to extract database information, including usernames, database names, and version details. This vulnerability is exploitable via the T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) tactic. To remediate, update to SIM-PKH 2.4.2 or later, and ensure the 'id' parameter is properly sanitized. Additionally, consider implementing a Web Application Firewall (WAF) to detect and prevent similar attacks.

items.title: CVE-2018-25409: SIM-PKH 2.4.1 contains an arbitrary file upload vulnerability that allows authen...
items.description: SIM-PKH 2.4.1 contains an arbitrary file upload vulnerability that allows authenticated attackers to upload malicious files by submitting PHP code through the fupload parameter. Attackers can upload PHP files via the aksi_pengurus.php endpoint with module=pengurus and act=update parameters, which are stored in the foto directory and executed as web scripts.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2018-25409 is a critical arbitrary file upload vulnerability in SIM-PKH 2.4.1, allowing authenticated attackers to upload malicious PHP files via the fupload parameter in the aksi_pengurus.php endpoint. This vulnerability can be exploited by submitting PHP code through the fupload parameter, which is stored in the foto directory and executed as web scripts. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to remote code execution (RCE), allowing attackers to gain unauthorized access to the system. To remediate this vulnerability, administrators should update to SIM-PKH 2.4.2 or later, which patches this issue. Additionally, it is recommended to restrict file uploads to specific, trusted sources and implement web application firewalls (WAFs) to detect and prevent suspicious traffic.

items.title: CVE-2018-25408: The Open ISES Project 3.30A contains a path traversal vulnerability in the ajax/...
items.description: The Open ISES Project 3.30A contains a path traversal vulnerability in the ajax/download.php endpoint that allows unauthenticated attackers to download arbitrary files by manipulating the filename parameter. Attackers can supply directory traversal sequences ../ in the filename parameter to access files outside the intended directory, including configuration files and system files.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2018-25408 is a path traversal vulnerability in the Open ISES Project 3.30A's ajax/download.php endpoint, allowing unauthenticated attackers to download arbitrary files by manipulating the filename parameter. Attackers can supply directory traversal sequences ../ to access files outside the intended directory, including configuration files and system files. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 7.5 and is exploitable via T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application). To remediate, update to version 3.30B or later, and ensure the 'allow_url_fopen' directive is set to 'Off' in the php.ini file. Additionally, restrict access to the ajax/download.php endpoint to authenticated users only.

items.description: A vulnerability was detected in TRENDnet TEW-432BRP 3.10B20. The affected element is the function formSetFirewallRule of the file /goform/formSetFirewallRule. The manipulation of the argument firewall_name results in stack-based buffer overflow. The attack can be executed remotely. The exploit is now public and may be used. The vendor explains: "This product has been EOL for 15 years (since 2009).

items.title: New Platform Uses Cryptographic Invisibility to Protect AI-Built Applications
items.description: Atsign’s AI Architect applies cryptographic protections to agentic software development, aiming to prevent attackers from exploiting vulnerabilities by making application identities effectively invisible. The post New Platform Uses Cryptographic Invisibility to Protect AI-Built Applications appeared first on SecurityWeek.

But the same challenges persist. Outages still last hours, causing significant financial losses, operational disruption, and reputational impact. Threat response and mean time to

items.title: Will AI Kill the Bug Bounty Industry?
items.description: Anthropic's Mythos is accelerating vulnerability discovery to machine speed, forcing the bug bounty industry and offensive security teams to adapt to a future where finding flaws is no longer the hard part. The post Will AI Kill the Bug Bounty Industry? appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: French govt messaging service breached in account hijacking attack
items.description: DINUM, the digital affairs directorate of the French government, warned that hackers used a hijacked user account to breach Tchap, the French government's encrypted messaging platform. [...]

items.title: New FROST Attack Lets Websites Track What Sites and Apps You Open via SSD Timing
items.description: A malicious website can work out which sites you visit and which apps you open, using nothing but JavaScript and the timing of your SSD. The attack, called FROST, needs no native code, no extension, and no permission prompt. You open the page, leave the tab sitting there, and it watches the drive for contention in the background. Researchers at Graz University of Technology built it and

items.title: Check Point VPN Zero-Day Exploited in Qilin Ransomware Attacks
items.description: The authentication bypass vulnerability allows attackers to establish VPN connections without a valid password. The post Check Point VPN Zero-Day Exploited in Qilin Ransomware Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Hades PyPI Attack: 19 Packages Poisoned to Auto-Run Bun Credential Stealer
items.description: The Miasma supply chain campaign has sparked a fresh attack wave called Hades, this time involving 37 malicious wheel artifacts across 19 packages in the Python Package Index (PyPI) registry, as the Mini Shai-Hulud-style attacks continue to be refined and splintered to target specific ecosystems. "The compromised releases shipped a *-setup.pth file that attempts to execute automatically

items.title: CISA gives feds 3 days to patch Check Point VPN bug exploited as zero-day
items.description: CISA has ordered U.S. government agencies to secure their Check Point Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments against a critical vulnerability exploited in zero-day attacks by Qilin ransomware affiliates. [...]

items.title: Google patches new Chrome zero-day flaw exploited in the wild
items.description: Google has released emergency updates to patch another Chrome zero-day vulnerability that has been exploited in the wild, the fifth such flaw patched since the start of the year. [...]

items.title: LiteLLM Flaw CVE-2026-42271 Exploited in the Wild, Chains to Unauthenticated RCE
items.description: The U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) on Monday added a high-severity flaw impacting BerriAI LiteLLM to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, citing evidence of active exploitation. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-42271 (CVSS score: 8.7), is a command injection vulnerability that could allow any authenticated user to run arbitrary commands on the

items.title: Google Patches 5th Chrome Zero-Day Exploited in 2026
items.description: The vulnerability is tracked as CVE-2026-11645 and it was reported in late April by an anonymous researcher. The post Google Patches 5th Chrome Zero-Day Exploited in 2026 appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 9th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9964, (Tue, Jun 9th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: NFCShare Android malware spreads via fake banking app updates on GitHub
items.description: New variants of the NFCShare Android malware are being distributed as fake updates for legitimate banking apps hosted on GitHub. [...]

items.title: SoFi confirms third-party data breach at Hong Kong subsidiary
items.description: SoFi Hong Kong is warning that it suffered a data breach after hackers gained access to a database at a third-party vendor containing customer information. [...]

items.title: New Apple feature automatically changes your compromised passwords
items.description: At WWDC 26, Apple announced an Apple Intelligence-powered feature that can automatically fix weak and compromised passwords. This works in Safari, and it's rolling out with iOS 27. [...]

items.title: Silent Ransom Group Hits US Law Firms in Escalating Extortion Attacks
items.description: The financially motivated group is combining vishing, IT impersonation, and in-person office intrusions to steal data and extort victims.

items.title: New Shai-Hulud attack trojanizes 19 science-focused PyPI packages
items.description: Hackers compromised 19 packages on the PyPI, collectively downloaded hundreds of thousands of times, in a new Shai-Hulud supply-chain attack that delivered malware designed to steal developer secrets. [...]

items.title: Check Point VPN Flaw Exploited Since Early May
items.description: A newly discovered, critical zero-day vulnerability is under attack; a Qilin ransomware affiliate has been blamed for at least one incident.

items.title: UK gives big tech 3 months to create device controls to block nude images of kids
items.description: The companies “must activate built-in features or implement technical solutions on smartphones and tablets to detect and block nude images for children,” according to a press release from the Home Office. Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced the measure in a speech at London Tech Week Monday.

items.title: One-Character Linux Kernel Flaw Enables Local Root Access, Exploits Now Public
items.description: Security researchers have published a detailed, working exploit for a Linux kernel use-after-free that lets an unprivileged local user escalate to root and break out of a container. The flaw, CVE-2026-23111, sits in the kernel's nf_tables packet-filtering code and was patched upstream on February 5, 2026. Exodus Intelligence released its full technical walkthrough on June 8, and it is not even

items.title: Iran Signed a Ceasefire — Its Hackers Didn't
items.description: An extension of the Geneva Conventions could impose restrictions on cyberwarfare under ceasefire conditions and close a major loophole in international conflict.

items.title: WhatsApp says it disrupted new NSO spyware phishing attacks
items.description: WhatsApp has detected and stopped spear-phishing campaigns allegedly conducted by the NSO Group after investigating user reports of social engineering attacks. [...]

items.title: A Security Raises $37 Million for Autonomous Offensive Security Platform
items.description: The company founded by Yossi Torati, Omer Gull, and Yuval Itzchakov has emerged from stealth mode. The post A Security Raises $37 Million for Autonomous Offensive Security Platform appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Armenia’s pro-Europe party wins election despite Russia-linked disinformation
items.description: Pashinyan's Civil Contract party won nearly 50% of Sunday's vote, defeating the pro-Russian Strong Armenia party led by Russian-Armenian billionaire Samvel Karapetyan, which received around 23% of the vote.

items.title: Meta Blocks NSO Group's New WhatsApp Phishing Attack, Files Contempt Order
items.description: Meta on Monday said it detected and blocked spear-phishing attempts linked to Israeli spyware vendor NSO Group. In addition, the tech giant said it's filing a federal court contempt order against the company for violating a permanent injunction that barred it from targeting WhatsApp and its users. "They tried to trick people into clicking on malicious links to drive them to external websites

items.title: TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign: Activity Through 2026-06-07, (Mon, Jun 8th)
items.description: This diary continues the Internet Storm Center&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s tracking of the TeamPCP supply chain campaign, first documented in the SANS white paper When the Security Scanner Became the Weapon and most recently in the handler diary Activity Through 2026-05-24. Since that update, the story moved into two new places: the United States government, which formally caught up to the campaign, and the wider population of attackers now wielding the Mini Shai-Hulud framework that TeamPCP open-sourced last month.

items.title: WhatsApp says NSO targeted users with spearfishing attacks in violation of court order
items.description: WhatsApp said it is filing a federal court contempt order against NSO for violating a permanent injunction that bars it from mounting attacks against its users.

items.title: Gogs patches critical zero-day enabling remote code execution
items.description: Gogs has patched a critical security zero-day flaw that can allow attackers to compromise Internet-facing instances and access any repositories (including private ones). [...]

items.title: 'Hades' Campaign Against PyPI Puts New Spin on Shai-Hulud
items.description: The latest attacks, which hit 37 PyPI wheels and 19 code packages, show a continued evolution of the persistent software supply chain threat.

items.title: Critical UniFi OS bug lets hackers gain root without authentication
items.description: Attackers can chain three already fixed vulnerabilities in the Ubiquiti UniFi OS server to execute remote code with root privileges and without authentication. [...]

items.title: Everybody Is Vibe Coding But Nobody Told the Security Team
items.description: AI-driven development is not something organizations can or should block. But it must be governed. The post Everybody Is Vibe Coding But Nobody Told the Security Team appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Critical Check Point VPN Flaw Exploited to Bypass Passwords in IKEv1 Setups
items.description: Check Point has warned of active exploitation of a critical vulnerability impacting Remote Access VPN and Mobile Access deployments that are configured to use the deprecated IKEv1 key exchange protocol. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-50751 (CVSS score: 9.3), is a case of a logic flow weakness in certificate validation that allows an unauthenticated remote attacker to bypass user

items.title: Russia upgrades rules for its digital spy system to better track citizens online
items.description: New regulations published by Russia's Ministry of Digital Development at the end of May updated the technical standards governing SORM, formally known as the System for Operative Investigative Activities.

items.title: WhatsApp Catches Spyware Firm NSO Defying No-Hacking Court Order
items.description: The Meta-owned communications app is filing a federal court contempt order against NSO. The post WhatsApp Catches Spyware Firm NSO Defying No-Hacking Court Order appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Cybersecurity M&A Roundup: 26 Deals Announced in May 2026
items.description: Significant cybersecurity M&A deals announced by Akamai, Check Point, Cisco, Cyera, Dragos, WatchGuard and Zscaler. The post Cybersecurity M&A Roundup: 26 Deals Announced in May 2026 appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Exposed Fuel Tank Gauges Under Attack in the US
items.description: Threat actors are taking advantage of Internet-exposed tank gauges by breaching gas stations, opening the door to disruption.

items.title: Adaptive, Agentic AI Worms Loom as Next Enterprise Threat
items.description: AI worms, or "viruses with wings and brains," adapt to new environments, seek out vulnerabilities, and will likely strike within a year, researchers say.

items.title: EU unveils tech sovereignty package to cut reliance on US, Chinese suppliers
items.description: The package bundles two draft laws — a Chips Act 2.0 and a Cloud and AI Development Act (CADA) — alongside an Open Source Strategy and a roadmap for digitalizing the energy system.

items.title: Trump AI Order Seeks Voluntary Frontier Model Testing
items.description: The White House's executive order establishes voluntary framework for early government access to frontier models while investing in federal security.

items.title: The Evil MSI Background is Back!, (Fri, Jun 5th)
items.description: A few months ago, I wrote a diary about a payload that was embedded into a JPEG picture. It was a MSI-branded background[1]. Yesterday, I spotted another one! It seems that the technic is getting more and more popular. This time, it started with a mail containing a WeTransfer link.

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Rust-Written IronWorm Hits NPM Supply Chain
items.description: Like Shai-Hulud, the campaign targets developers to steal credentials and reuses them to propagate across the software supply channel.

items.title: China's TA4922 Expands Cybercrime Attacks Globally
items.description: One of the world's most diverse, least-focused cybercrime groups is enlarging its footprint beyond East Asia.

items.title: 4 Critical Threats Where Attackers Have the Advantage
items.description: Gartner analysts issued a call to action to bolster defenses against several emerging critical threats, such as deepfakes and prompt injections.

items.title: Microsoft's Coreutils for Windows, (Thu, Jun 4th)
items.description: I&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;ve been using the GnuWin32 CoreUtils for Windows for many years now (it gives you many *nix core commands on Windows).

items.title: Continuing Scans for swagger.json, (Wed, Jun 3rd)
items.description: Enterprise applications often still use complex standards like SOAP for web services. The big advantage of SOAP is its tight and extensive standards, which enable interoperability across an enterprise governed by web services. The disadvantage of SOAP: First, while it is de facto usually used over HTTP, it does not leverage HTTP, leading to unnecessary complexity. Secondly, kids don&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t RTFM, and developers these days tend not to appreciate the art of careful system design; they rather throw code at an IDE to see what sticks, if they don&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;t vibe code it anyway.

items.title: CISA Urges Stronger Security for Automatic Tank Gauge Systems
New Wave Of Phishing Emails with SVG Files, (Tue, Jun 2nd)
items.description: For a few days, my SANS ISC mailbox is flooded with emails that delivers SVG files. An SVG ("Scalable Vector Graphic") is a web-friendly vector file format used for graphics and icons. No URL in the body, just â€œan imageâ€, that&#x27s the perfect way to deliver some malicious content. This isn&#x27t the first time that we see this technique used by threat actors[1].

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
items.description: A 24-year-old British national and senior member of the cybercrime group "Scattered Spider" has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft. Tyler Robert Buchanan admitted his role in a series of text-message phishing attacks in the summer of 2022 that allowed the group to hack into at least a dozen major technology companies and steal tens of millions of dollars worth of cryptocurrency from investors.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, April 2026 Edition
items.description: Microsoft today pushed software updates to fix a staggering 167 security vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and related software, including a SharePoint Server zero-day and a publicly disclosed weakness in Windows Defender dubbed "BlueHammer." Separately, Google Chrome fixed its fourth zero-day of 2026, and an emergency update for Adobe Reader nixes an actively exploited flaw that can lead to remote code execution.

items.title: CISA Issues Updated RESURGE Malware Analysis Highlighting a Stealthy but Active Threat
Immediate Action Required: CISA Issues Emergency Directive to Secure Cisco SD-WAN Systems
